steps.text: You read and created bar graphs with scale of 10. You figured out values at the lines AND between the lines. Let's look at some patterns you discovered.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong graph on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the lines on each graph's axis.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong graph on second attempt

steps.text: Let's think about this together. The scale of 1 has 20 lines. The scale of 10 has only 2 lines.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong graph three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Scale of 10 graph highlights. Tick marks at 0, 10, 20 pulse to show only 2 lines needed.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. The scale of 10 uses the fewest lines. Count them: 0, 10, 20. That's just 2 tick marks to show 20. The other graphs need more lines because their scales are smaller.

incorrects.description: Student selected Blue, which shows 20 in both graphs, not 35

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Blue shows 20. We need the bar that reaches 35 on the axis.

incorrects.description: Student selected Yellow, which shows 50 in both graphs, not 35

steps.text: Here's a hint: Yellow reaches 50 on the axis. Look for the bar that stops at 35 instead.

incorrects.description: Student selected Green, which shows 30 in both graphs, not 35

steps.text: You're working on it. Green reaches 30. Count the lines carefully to find which bar goes to 35.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Red bar in vertical graph highlights, showing it reaches the 35 line on the axis
Red bar in horizontal graph highlights, showing it reaches the 35 line on the axis

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. In the vertical graph, the Red bar goes up to the line marked 35. In the horizontal graph, the Red bar goes across to that same 35 mark. Red shows 35 in both.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'Counting items one by one', which does not benefit from scale of 10

params.description: Individual counting image highlights to show why this doesn't need scale of 10.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Counting items one by one works fine without grouping by tens. Where would grouping by tens actually help?

incorrects.description: Student selected at least one wrong option or missed a correct option on first attempt

params.description: Money image pulses to draw attention to grouping by tens.
Ruler image pulses to draw attention to marks in tens.

steps.text: Here's a hint: think about situations where you naturally group things by tens or skip count by tens.

incorrects.description: Student selected at least one wrong option or missed a correct option on second attempt

params.description: Money image highlights showing stacks organized by tens.
Ruler image highlights showing measurement marks in tens.

steps.text: You're working on it. Money comes in stacks of tens. Rulers have marks at 10, 20, 30. Both use scale of 10 because the numbers are bigger.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Money image highlights as system demonstrates correct answer.
Ruler image highlights as system demonstrates correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Money and measuring both use tens because they deal with bigger numbers. You count money in stacks of 10 dollar bills. You measure with a ruler marked at 10, 20, 30. Counting items one by one doesn't need grouping by tens. Select money and ruler.

condition.selected: I picked the line closest to the top of the bar

incorrects.description: Student selected closest line strategy, which doesn't use the halfway pattern

steps.text: The closest line is 50, but the bar shows 45. That strategy gives you an estimate, not the exact value. What pattern did we discover about halfway between the lines?

condition.selected: I added the two numbers on the lines

incorrects.description: Student selected adding strategy, which is incorrect for finding halfway

steps.text: Adding 40 and 50 gives 90, not 45. Think about the pattern we found. When the scale is 10, how much more is halfway between any two lines?

incorrects.description: Student selected guessing, missing the halfway pattern

steps.text: You don't need to guess. We learned a pattern. When the scale is 10, halfway between any two lines is always 5 more than the lower line. That's the strategy.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Ghost gridline at 45 pulses. Visual shows 40 to halfway equals 5 more.

steps.text: Here's the pattern. The scale is 10, so each big jump is 10. Half of 10 is 5. That means halfway between any two lines is 5 more than the lower line. The bar is at 45, halfway between 40 and 50. The answer is: I found halfway. That's always 5 more.
You learned to read values at the lines and between the lines. You used half of 10 is 5 to figure out tricky values.
That pattern finding will help you when we look at all the scales together next time. You'll be able to pick the best one for any data.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph with single bar at 45 remains visible.
